title: When Tech Support Speaks to You Like You're Slow
dek: *That tech support agent didn't make you angry because they were slow or unhelpful. They made you angry because their tone signaled you don't deserve full personhood in this transaction, and your nervous system registered the threat before your thinking brain caught up. Here's what that anger is trying to tell you, and what to do with the information.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Being spoken to like you're incompetent triggers anger because it signals a dominance violation, not because you're oversensitive. Your body reads condescension as a boundary intrusion that requires metabolic correction through assertion. Learning to track the physical signature of this anger before you respond changes everything about the interaction.

Most people think anger at condescension is a character flaw, a sign they're thin-skinned or ego-driven. But anger in these moments is actually your organism protecting something far more fundamental than pride. When someone speaks to you like you're slow, your body reads it as a dominance challenge, a test of whether you'll accept a lower position in the social hierarchy. The anger isn't irrational. It's your biology refusing to let someone else define your worth without your consent. ## The Body Knows Before You Do You're three sentences into the call when you feel it. Heat in your chest. A tightness behind your eyes. Your jaw sets without you telling it to. The tech support agent just asked if you've tried turning it off and on again, in that particular tone that suggests they think you're calling because you forgot where the power button lives. Pat Ogden's work in sensorimotor psychotherapy emphasizes that the body processes relational threats before the cortex gets involved. Your nervous system has already clocked the condescension, catalogued it as a boundary violation, and begun preparing a response while you're still trying to be polite. The anger you feel isn't a thought that produces a sensation. It's a sensation that will eventually produce thoughts, and those thoughts will be shaped by whether you can track what's happening in your body right now. In my own practice, I've watched people describe this moment with remarkable consistency. The heat rises from the solar plexus. The shoulders pull back slightly. The breath gets shallow and held. Your body is preparing to assert itself, to push back against the invisible hand trying to press you down into a smaller shape. This is adaptive. This is your organism saying: I will not be diminished without a fight. ## Why Condescension Lands So Hard Harriet Lerner observed that anger often functions as a messenger, carrying information about boundary violations we haven't yet consciously registered. When someone speaks to you like you're incompetent, they're not just being rude. They're performing a kind of social mathematics, calculating that they can afford to treat you this way because the power differential allows it. Your anger is the part of you that refuses to agree with their calculation. The sting isn't really about the specific words. It's about what those words imply about your position. You're supposed to be grateful for their help. You're supposed to accept that they know better. You're supposed to tolerate the subtle message that your time, your intelligence, and your lived experience of the problem count for less than their script and their assumptions. Over the years I've seen people tie themselves in knots trying to be "the bigger person" in these interactions, suppressing the anger because they've been taught that feeling it makes them petty. But PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load suggests that chronically suppressing justified anger carries measurable physiological costs. Your body doesn't care about your self-image as someone who never gets rattled. It cares about metabolizing the threat response that condescension triggers. ## The Metabolic Reality of Being Dismissed Gabor Maté points out that anger is fundamentally about self-protection, about maintaining the integrity of the self against forces that would fragment or diminish it. When you're spoken to like you're slow, your autonomic nervous system interprets this as a form of attack. Not a physical one, but an attack on your social standing, your competence, your right to be met as an equal. Your body responds accordingly. Cortisol and adrenaline spike. Your heart rate elevates. Blood flow redirects to large muscle groups in preparation for action. All of this happens because your organism is treating the condescension as a survival issue, which in ancestral environments it was. Being relegated to low status in a social group could mean reduced access to resources, mates, protection. Your anger is your body's refusal to accept that relegation without pushing back. The problem is that modern contexts don't allow for the physical assertion your body is preparing. You can't actually fight the tech support agent. You probably need them to solve your problem. So the activation sits in your system with nowhere to go, cycling and intensifying, making you feel both furious and trapped. This is where most people either explode inappropriately or collapse into a kind of resentful compliance that leaves them feeling worse. ## What Your Throat Wants to Say Notice what happens in your throat when you're swallowing your anger at condescension. There's often a thickness there, a sensation of words piling up behind your teeth, wanting out but blocked by some internal editor who's calculating whether it's safe or smart to say them. Your throat knows what it wants to say. It wants to name what's happening. "Don't speak to me that way." "I'm not an idiot." "Your tone suggests you think I haven't already tried the obvious solutions." These are boundary-setting statements, assertions of your right to be treated with basic respect. The anger in your body is generating the energy you'd need to deliver those statements with the force required to change the other person's behavior. But most of us have been trained to see that kind of directness as rude, aggressive, or unprofessional. So we perform a kind of internal violence instead, clamping down on our own throat, forcing ourselves to stay pleasant and cooperative while our nervous system screams that something here needs to be corrected. You can feel this as a literal tightness, sometimes for hours after the call ends. ## The Five-Minute Body Reset Here's something you can do in the moment or immediately after, ideally before the activation hardens into a headache or a knot between your shoulder blades. Stand up if you can. Plant your feet hip-width apart. Take a full breath in through your nose, then exhale with sound, a sigh or a hum that lets your throat vibrate. Do this three times. You're giving your throat permission to make noise, to push air out, which is a micro-version of the assertion your body wanted to make. Then shake. Literally shake your hands, your arms, bounce on your feet a little. This helps complete the mobilization response your nervous system initiated. You prepared to push back; now you're giving your body a way to discharge that preparation without directing it at anyone. Finally, place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Feel yourself breathing. Say out loud, even if you're alone: "I'm allowed to be angry when someone speaks to me like that." You're not committing to any particular action. You're just acknowledging that the anger is legitimate, that it's carrying information worth listening to. In our experience, this simple acknowledgment often takes half the charge out of the activation. Some people find that magnesium supplementation helps with the physical tension that accompanies chronic anger suppression. [Momentous Magtein Magnesium L-Threonate, 30 Servings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09WWK4R2C?tag=spankyspinola-20) supports the kind of nervous system regulation that makes it easier to feel your anger without being hijacked by it. Similarly, [Doctor's Best L-Theanine with Suntheanine 150 mg](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001OXTGVG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can take the edge off the activation without numbing you to what the anger is trying to communicate. ## What Assertion Actually Looks Like Most people think their only options are swallow it or blow up. But there's a third path that requires tracking your body's signals closely enough to catch the anger while it's still information rather than explosion. Assertion in these moments sounds like: "I appreciate your help, and I need you to know I've already tried the basic troubleshooting steps. Can we move to the next level of diagnosis?" You're not attacking the person. You're calmly correcting the power dynamic they tried to establish. You're refusing to accept the diminished position without being aggressive about it. This is harder than it sounds because it requires you to stay connected to your anger as a source of clarity rather than letting it flood you. You're using the energy the anger provides to fuel a boundary, not a blow-up. You're letting your body's "no" shape your words without letting the activation take over your prefrontal cortex. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this distinction. Anger that you're aware of and can track in your body becomes information you can use. Anger that's suppressed or unconscious becomes a force that uses you, pushing you into reactions you later regret or into a collapse that leaves you feeling helpless. ## The Pattern You're Really Angry About Here's what makes tech support condescension so particularly enraging: it's often not the first time today, this week, this month that someone has spoken to you this way. The anger you feel on this call is carrying the accumulated weight of every other time you've been patronized, dismissed, explained to, or treated like your competence needs to be verified before you're granted basic respect. You're not overreacting to this one interaction. You're having a proportional response to a pattern, and your body knows the difference even if your conscious mind is trying to convince you that you're being too sensitive. The tech support agent is just the current face on a dynamic you've encountered in a hundred different contexts. This is why the anger can feel so big, so out of proportion to the actual stakes of getting your printer fixed or your account opened. The stakes aren't really about the printer. They're about whether you're going to spend your whole life accommodating people who feel entitled to speak down to you, or whether you're going to start pushing back in ways that change the pattern. For a deeper exploration of how the body holds these patterns and how to work with them directly, [Sensorimotor Psychotherapy - Pat Ogden](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0393708810?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a rigorous framework that many people find changing. You can also explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) for tools that help you map your own anger patterns with more precision. ## Teaching People How to Speak to You Every time you let condescension pass without comment, you're training that person that this is an acceptable way to speak to you. Every time you suppress your anger and perform cheerful cooperation, you're reinforcing the dynamic that made you angry in the first place. This doesn't mean you have to be combative. It means you have to be clear. When you feel that heat in your chest, that's your cue to pause, take a breath, and decide whether this is a moment where you're going to let your anger inform a boundary. Sometimes the answer is no, the stakes are too low or the cost too high. But sometimes the answer is yes, and in those moments your anger is offering you the exact energy you need to say what needs saying. The people who respect you most are often the ones you've been willing to correct. The relationships that feel most mutual are the ones where you've asserted yourself when something felt off. Your anger at being spoken down to is trying to move you toward that kind of clarity, toward interactions where you're met as a peer rather than patronized as a problem. ## When the Anger Is Bigger Than the Moment Sometimes you notice that your reaction to a condescending tech support agent is so intense it surprises even you. You're shaking. You're close to tears. You want to scream or throw the phone or write a ten-page complaint letter that you know you'll never send. When the anger is bigger than the moment, it's usually because the moment is activating something older. A parent who spoke to you like you were stupid. A teacher who dismissed your questions. A boss who made you feel small. The current condescension is real, and it's also functioning as a trigger for a wound that never fully healed. This is worth paying attention to, not to invalidate your anger at the current situation but to understand why it's landing with such force. Your body is trying to tell you something about what you still need to heal, what you still need to assert, what you still need to say no to. The anger is giving you a map to your own unfinished business. You can find [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore how to work with this kind of layered anger, how to separate the current trigger from the historical wound without dismissing either one. The practice is learning to let your anger be specific to the moment while also acknowledging the deeper pattern it's pointing toward. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it unprofessional to show anger when someone speaks to me condescendingly at work? There's a difference between showing anger and using anger as information to set a boundary. You don't have to raise your voice or lose your composure to assert yourself. A calm statement like "I'd appreciate a different tone" or "I'm familiar with the basics, let's move forward" uses the clarity your anger provides without performing the emotion itself. Professional doesn't mean doormat. ### What if I'm wrong and they weren't actually being condescending? Your body's response is data, not a court verdict. If you felt condescended to, something in their tone or words triggered that read, and that's worth honoring even if their intent was neutral. You can assert a boundary gently. If you misread, they'll clarify and you'll both move on. If you read it right, you've just changed the dynamic in a way that serves you. ### How do I stop ruminating about condescending interactions hours or days later? The rumination is often your mind trying to find the response your body wanted to make but didn't. Write it out. Say out loud what you wish you'd said. Move your body to discharge the activation that's still stuck. The loop breaks when you give the anger somewhere to go other than round and round in your head. ## The Anger That Protects Your Personhood You're going to have more interactions with people who speak to you like you're slow. Tech support agents, customer service reps, clerks, colleagues, strangers on the internet. The condescension is a feature of hierarchical systems that need someone to be on the bottom, and sometimes you're going to be cast in that role whether you consent to it or not. Your anger at those moments is not a flaw in your character. It's your personhood refusing to be erased. It's the part of you that knows you deserve to be spoken to with basic respect, that your time and intelligence and lived experience matter, that you're not obligated to shrink yourself to make someone else's job easier. That anger is trying to protect something essential. The question is whether you'll let it. Whether you'll track it in your body, use it as information, allow it to shape boundaries that change how people speak to you. Or whether you'll keep swallowing it, keep performing the pleasant compliance that leaves you feeling diminished and resentful, keep telling yourself that being angry about condescension means you're too sensitive or too proud. You can learn more about the larger framework we're working with at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and if you're curious about the person behind these words, [the about page](/about) offers some context. But the real work happens in your body, in those moments when you feel the heat rising and you have to decide what to do with it. That's where your anger becomes either a force that shapes your life toward more dignity, or a force you spend your energy suppressing. The choice, as always, is yours. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on when tech support speaks to you like you're slow, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
